content:
This expansion explains why contradiction matters in practice, details the ethical and operational risks, and gives concrete, prioritized steps leaders can take to restore trust and protect respondents. The goal is to move from a technically misleading promise to a defensible, transparent practice that preserves respondent safety and data integrity.

The Illusion of Anonymity

“After a course, participants are given a survey of 10 questions. To complete the survey, participants open a URL in their web browser, available for a short period, and log in with their organization username and password. The survey prompts for the course name, but not their name. Instead, the survey description on this page indicates that all answers and the survey itself will remain anonymous.”

Summary of Problem

This expansion explains why that contradiction matters in practice, details the ethical and operational risks, and gives concrete, prioritized steps leaders can take to restore trust and protect respondents. The goal is to move from a technically misleading promise to a defensible, transparent practice that preserves respondent safety and data integrity.

Ethical and Practical Consequences

False or Misleading Anonymity. When a survey requires a login but claims anonymity, the statement is technically inaccurate. Authentication systems create persistent metadata — login records, session identifiers, timestamps, and IP addresses — that can be used to link responses back to individuals. That gap between promise and capability is not merely semantic; it breaches informed participation and can be experienced by respondents as deception.

Identifiability and Re‑identification Risk. Even if the survey form does not ask for names, system logs and enrollment lists create a re‑identification pathway. For example, a short response window combined with a small class roster makes it trivial to match a timestamped login to a single student. The risk grows when logs are retained, accessible to multiple administrators, or combined with other institutional datasets.

Coercion, Power Imbalance, and Data Quality. Students who believe their feedback could be traced may self‑censor or avoid participation. That produces biased, low‑value data and undermines the survey’s stated purpose. Instructors and administrators who rely on such compromised feedback risk making poor decisions about course redesign or personnel evaluation.

Inadequate Informed Consent and Transparency. The survey description omits critical facts: what metadata is collected, who can access it, how long it is retained, and whether responses may be repurposed. Without that information, consent is not meaningful. Respondents must be able to weigh the privacy risks before participating.

Additional Technical and Governance Risks

• Data retention and minimization failures - Storing raw logs, IP addresses, or unaggregated responses increases exposure and the attack surface for breaches. • Security vulnerabilities - Weak encryption, poor access controls, or shared admin credentials can lead to unauthorized disclosure. • Sampling bias and accessibility - Short availability windows and mandatory single‑sign‑on can include students who lack reliable access or who are off-site. • Lack of auditability - Without independent verification, claims of anonymity are unverifiable and therefore untrustworthy. • Fabrication and deepfake threats - screenshots, doctored logs, or synthetic media can be weaponized to fabricate evidence of wrongdoing or to attribute responses falsely. Institutions must plan for detection and response.

Prioritized Recommendations for Executive Management

Below are practical, prioritized actions that balance feasibility with ethical impact. Each item is actionable and can be implemented in phases.

  1. Separate Authentication from Response Collection (High priority) • Implement one‑time anonymous tokens. Authenticate only to confirm eligibility, then issue a single‑use token that grants access to the survey without linking responses to identities. Immediately discard the mapping between token and user. • Operational note: Use a short token issuance window and cryptographically secure random tokens to prevent token guessing.

  2. Provide Clear, Written Informed Consent (High priority) • Add a concise privacy notice at the survey entry that lists collected items, retention periods, who can access results, and intended uses. Require respondents to acknowledge the notice before proceeding.

  3. Minimize Data and Limit Retention (Medium priority) • Avoid storing IPs, timestamps, or login metadata with responses. If logs are necessary for security, separate them from response data and enforce strict deletion schedules. • Automate deletion of raw logs and unaggregated responses after a short, documented retention period.

  4. Strengthen Technical Safeguards (Medium priority) • Encrypt data at rest and in transit. • Apply role‑based access controls and MFA for administrators. • Maintain immutable audit logs** for access to survey systems and publish summaries of audit findings.

  5. Enforce Aggregation and Fair Relabeling Policies (Medium priority) • Report only aggregated results and enforce minimum group sizes before publishing subgroup data. • Prohibit repurposing survey data for instructor evaluation or accreditation without documented review and explicit consent.

  6. Commission Independent Audit and Prepare for Fabrication (Lower priority but important) • Engage a privacy officer or external auditor to verify anonymization claims and review communications. Publish a summary of findings. • Create a deepfake and fabrication response plan that includes chain‑of‑custody procedures for logs and forensic checks for media.

Ethical Options If Management Rejects Recommendations

If leadership resists, staff and privacy advocates should pursue these ethical options: • Document concerns formally with a dated memo that outlines risks and recommended mitigations. • Propose low‑cost compromises such as an explicit privacy notice, an extended response window, and a deletion schedule. • Escalate to oversight bodies, including the privacy officer, ethics committee, or data governance board. • Refuse to endorse misleading reports that claim anonymity when it cannot be guaranteed. • Insist on pre‑aggregated datasets for analysis that meet minimum group thresholds.

Practical Checklist

• Authenticate then anonymize using one‑time tokens. • Inform clearly with explicit limits of anonymity. • Minimize collected data and avoid unnecessary metadata. • Limit retention with automated deletion. • Aggregate safely and enforce minimum group sizes. • Audit regularly and publish summaries. • Secure storage with encryption and RBAC. • Prepare for fabrication with verification procedures. • Prevent unfair relabeling through policy and consent. • Communicate outcomes and publish follow‑up action plans.

Conclusion

Requiring authentication while promising anonymity creates a structural contradiction that erodes trust and can enable misuse. The ethical path is to be transparent about technical limits, to separate eligibility checks from response collection, to minimize and promptly delete identifying metadata, and to govern survey data with clear policies and independent oversight. Implementing the prioritized steps above will restore respondent trust, improve data quality, and reduce legal and reputational risk. Designing surveys ethically is not an obstacle to decision-making; it is the foundation for reliable, actionable insights that genuinely improve courses while protecting the people who provide the feedback.

Mathjax

Las ecuaciones matemáticas se pueden renderizar usando la sintaxis de Mathjax con soporte de símbolos AMS.

Opcionalmente, habilita esto por página agregando mathjax: true a tu frontmatter.

Luego, usa $$ ... $$ en una línea por sí sola para renderizar una ecuación de bloque:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

La versión sin procesar es:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con \\( ... \\) , como \( x^n / y \) . ¡Es fácil!

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con `\\( ... \\)` , como \\( x^n / y \\) . ¡Es fácil!

Mathjax

Les équations mathématiques peuvent être rendues en utilisant la syntaxe Mathjax avec le support des symboles AMS.

Activez ceci de manière optionnelle page par page en ajoutant mathjax: true à votre frontmatter.

Ensuite, utilisez $$ ... $$ sur une ligne par elle-même pour rendre une équation de bloc:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

La version brute est:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Écrivez des équations en ligne avec \\( ... \\) , comme \( x^n / y \) . C’est facile!

Los emoji se pueden habilitar en un proyecto Hugo de varias maneras. :zap:

Soporte de Emoji

La función emojify se puede llamar directamente en las plantillas o Shortcodes en línea.

Para habilitar emoji globalmente, establece enableEmoji en true en la configuración de tu sitio y luego puedes escribir códigos abreviados de emoji directamente en los archivos de contenido; por ejemplo:

La Hoja de trucos de Emoji es una referencia útil para los códigos abreviados de emoji.

N.B. Los pasos anteriores habilitan los caracteres y secuencias de emoji del Estándar Unicode en Hugo, sin embargo, la representación de estos glifos depende del navegador y la plataforma. Para estilizar los emoji, puedes usar una fuente de emoji de terceros o una pila de fuentes; por ejemplo:

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Soporte de Emoji

content:
Les emoji peuvent être activés dans un projet Hugo de plusieurs façons. :zap:

Support des Emoji

La fonction emojify peut être appelée directement dans les modèles ou les Shortcodes en ligne.

Pour activer les emoji globalement, définissez enableEmoji sur true dans la configuration de votre site, puis vous pouvez taper les codes abrégés d’emoji directement dans les fichiers de contenu; par exemple :

La Feuille de triche Emoji est une référence utile pour les codes abrégés d’emoji.

N.B. Les étapes ci-dessus activent les caractères et séquences emoji du standard Unicode dans Hugo, cependant le rendu de ces glyphes dépend du navigateur et de la plateforme. Pour styliser les emoji, vous pouvez utiliser une police emoji tierce ou une pile de polices; par exemple :

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

content:
Face Value: When Shopping Malls Secretly Read Our Faces — Ethics, Governance, and Soft Ethics in Practice

Introduction

Cadillac Fairview’s covert use of facial recognition inside mall directory kiosks is a vivid example of how biometric surveillance, weak consent practices, and opaque vendor relationships can collide with basic privacy expectations. This blog post examines that case through the lens of AI governance, ethical AI frameworks, and soft ethics, and draws practical lessons for data scientists, designers, and policy makers. It argues that technical safeguards alone are insufficient: meaningful consent, transparency, accountability, and purpose limitation must be embedded into both design and organizational practice.

“Cadillac Fairview embedded cameras inside their digital information kiosks at 12 shopping malls across Canada and used facial recognition technology without their customers’ knowledge or consent.” - OPC

What is the importance of this case?

  • Real-world stakes: The deployment collected roughly five million images and generated biometric representations of shoppers, creating long-term identity risks.
  • Ethics concepts at play: privacy; consent; biometric data sensitivity; transparency; accountability; data minimization; purpose limitation; vendor governance; soft ethics.
  • Core tension: Organizations may claim analytics are “non-identifying” while producing sensitive biometric derivatives that are inherently personal.

Ethical framing: Which principles apply?

This section aligns the case with established ethical AI concepts and frameworks.

  1. Privacy and biometric sensitivity Biometric data (facial features, gait, iris patterns) is uniquely identifying and effectively permanent. Ethical frameworks treat biometric information as highly sensitive, requiring stronger protections than ordinary personal data.
  2. Informed and meaningful consent Consent must be express, informed, and specific. Small decals or buried privacy policies do not meet the threshold for meaningful consent when biometric capture is involved.
  3. Transparency and explainability Transparency requires clear notice about what is collected, how it is processed, who has access, and retention policies. Explainability extends to how analytics produce inferences (e.g., age, gender) and what uncertainties or biases those inferences carry.
  4. Accountability and governance Organizations must know and control what their vendors do with data. Accountability includes audits, contractual controls, and the ability to demonstrate compliance with privacy laws and ethical commitments.
  5. Data minimization and purpose limitation Collect only what is necessary and retain it only for a justified purpose. Generating and storing biometric representations without a clear, limited purpose violates these principles.

Soft ethics: Why voluntary norms still matter

Regulation often lags behind technology. Soft ethics—codes of conduct, industry standards, design norms, and public commitments—can fill gaps when laws are silent or ambiguous.

  • Codes and pledges: Public commitments to avoid biometric surveillance without consent can shape corporate behavior.
  • Design norms: Defaulting to privacy-preserving designs (e.g., on-device aggregation, ephemeral analytics) reduces risk.
  • Stakeholder engagement: Consulting affected communities and privacy experts before deployment builds legitimacy.
  • Internal governance: Ethics review boards, vendor due diligence, and impact assessments operationalize soft ethics. Soft ethics are not a substitute for law, but they can reduce harm and build trust while regulation catches up.

Technical and organizational controls that should have been used

Below are practical controls mapped to the ethical failures in the case.

  • Notice and consent mechanisms
  • Express opt-in for biometric analytics; clear signage and an accessible consent flow.
  • Data minimization
  • Aggregate counts only; avoid storing raw images or biometric templates.
  • Vendor governance
  • Contracts requiring deletion, audit rights, and breach notification.
  • Transparency tools
  • Public documentation of models, retention periods, and data flows.
  • Accountability measures
  • Independent audits; privacy impact assessments (PIAs); ethics review board sign-off.
  • Technical privacy
  • On-device processing; differential privacy for aggregated outputs; encryption in transit and at rest.

Bias, fairness, and explainability — downstream harms

Even when the stated goal is innocuous (age and gender analytics), automated inferences can be biased and inaccurate across demographic groups. Misestimation of age or gender can lead to discriminatory targeting or exclusionary practices. Explainability matters because stakeholders must understand the limits and error rates of these inferences before they are used to make decisions.

The Cadillac Fairview example: what went wrong

Key failures identified by privacy regulators include:

  • Lack of meaningful consent — decals on doors were insufficient notice for biometric capture.
  • Opaque vendor practices — biometric representations were stored by a third party in a centralized database, and the company claimed ignorance of that storage.
  • Retention without purpose — five million biometric representations were retained “for no discernable reason.”

These failures illustrate how weak governance and poor transparency can convert a seemingly benign analytics project into a major privacy breach.

A short checklist for ethical deployment of biometric analytics

  • Purpose clarity — Document why biometric analytics are necessary and what decisions they will inform.
  • Consent design — Implement explicit, granular opt-ins; avoid implied consent.
  • Vendor controls — Require contractual deletion, no secondary use, and audit rights.
  • Minimize retention — Store only aggregated results; delete raw and derived biometric data promptly.
  • Impact assessment — Conduct a PIA and algorithmic impact assessment before deployment.
  • Public transparency — Publish a plain-language summary of the system, data flows, and safeguards.
  • Independent oversight — Use external audits and ethics review boards for high-risk systems.

Broader governance lessons

  • Regulation and enforcement matter. Privacy commissioners intervened and the company removed the cameras; enforcement is a critical backstop.
  • Organizational awareness is essential. Companies must know what their vendors do; “we didn’t know” is not an adequate defense.
  • Public trust is fragile. Once trust is broken, reputational damage and regulatory scrutiny follow, and future legitimate uses of analytics become harder.

Conclusion

The Cadillac Fairview case is a cautionary tale: even analytics framed as aggregate or “non-identifying” can produce sensitive biometric artifacts that demand the highest ethical scrutiny. Ethical AI frameworks, robust governance, and soft ethics practices together form a layered defense against misuse. For practitioners, the takeaway is clear: embed privacy by design, insist on meaningful consent, govern vendors tightly, and treat biometric outputs as permanently sensitive. Doing so protects individuals and preserves the social license organizations need to innovate responsibly. I believe this practice will immediately alleviate the fear of privacy concerns.

This expansion explains why contradiction matters in practice, details the ethical and operational risks, and gives concrete, prioritized steps leaders can take to restore trust and protect respondents. The goal is to move from a technically misleading promise to a defensible, transparent practice that preserves respondent safety and data integrity.

The Illusion of Anonymity

“After a course, participants are given a survey of 10 questions. To complete the survey, participants open a URL in their web browser, available for a short period, and log in with their organization username and password. The survey prompts for the course name, but not their name. Instead, the survey description on this page indicates that all answers and the survey itself will remain anonymous.”

Summary of Problem

This expansion explains why that contradiction matters in practice, details the ethical and operational risks, and gives concrete, prioritized steps leaders can take to restore trust and protect respondents. The goal is to move from a technically misleading promise to a defensible, transparent practice that preserves respondent safety and data integrity.

Ethical and Practical Consequences

False or Misleading Anonymity. When a survey requires a login but claims anonymity, the statement is technically inaccurate. Authentication systems create persistent metadata — login records, session identifiers, timestamps, and IP addresses — that can be used to link responses back to individuals. That gap between promise and capability is not merely semantic; it breaches informed participation and can be experienced by respondents as deception.

Identifiability and Re‑identification Risk. Even if the survey form does not ask for names, system logs and enrollment lists create a re‑identification pathway. For example, a short response window combined with a small class roster makes it trivial to match a timestamped login to a single student. The risk grows when logs are retained, accessible to multiple administrators, or combined with other institutional datasets.

Coercion, Power Imbalance, and Data Quality. Students who believe their feedback could be traced may self‑censor or avoid participation. That produces biased, low‑value data and undermines the survey’s stated purpose. Instructors and administrators who rely on such compromised feedback risk making poor decisions about course redesign or personnel evaluation.

Inadequate Informed Consent and Transparency. The survey description omits critical facts: what metadata is collected, who can access it, how long it is retained, and whether responses may be repurposed. Without that information, consent is not meaningful. Respondents must be able to weigh the privacy risks before participating.

Additional Technical and Governance Risks

• Data retention and minimization failures - Storing raw logs, IP addresses, or unaggregated responses increases exposure and the attack surface for breaches. • Security vulnerabilities - Weak encryption, poor access controls, or shared admin credentials can lead to unauthorized disclosure. • Sampling bias and accessibility - Short availability windows and mandatory single‑sign‑on can include students who lack reliable access or who are off-site. • Lack of auditability - Without independent verification, claims of anonymity are unverifiable and therefore untrustworthy. • Fabrication and deepfake threats - screenshots, doctored logs, or synthetic media can be weaponized to fabricate evidence of wrongdoing or to attribute responses falsely. Institutions must plan for detection and response.

Prioritized Recommendations for Executive Management

Below are practical, prioritized actions that balance feasibility with ethical impact. Each item is actionable and can be implemented in phases.

  1. Separate Authentication from Response Collection (High priority) • Implement one‑time anonymous tokens. Authenticate only to confirm eligibility, then issue a single‑use token that grants access to the survey without linking responses to identities. Immediately discard the mapping between token and user. • Operational note: Use a short token issuance window and cryptographically secure random tokens to prevent token guessing.

  2. Provide Clear, Written Informed Consent (High priority) • Add a concise privacy notice at the survey entry that lists collected items, retention periods, who can access results, and intended uses. Require respondents to acknowledge the notice before proceeding.

  3. Minimize Data and Limit Retention (Medium priority) • Avoid storing IPs, timestamps, or login metadata with responses. If logs are necessary for security, separate them from response data and enforce strict deletion schedules. • Automate deletion of raw logs and unaggregated responses after a short, documented retention period.

  4. Strengthen Technical Safeguards (Medium priority) • Encrypt data at rest and in transit. • Apply role‑based access controls and MFA for administrators. • Maintain immutable audit logs** for access to survey systems and publish summaries of audit findings.

  5. Enforce Aggregation and Fair Relabeling Policies (Medium priority) • Report only aggregated results and enforce minimum group sizes before publishing subgroup data. • Prohibit repurposing survey data for instructor evaluation or accreditation without documented review and explicit consent.

  6. Commission Independent Audit and Prepare for Fabrication (Lower priority but important) • Engage a privacy officer or external auditor to verify anonymization claims and review communications. Publish a summary of findings. • Create a deepfake and fabrication response plan that includes chain‑of‑custody procedures for logs and forensic checks for media.

Ethical Options If Management Rejects Recommendations

If leadership resists, staff and privacy advocates should pursue these ethical options: • Document concerns formally with a dated memo that outlines risks and recommended mitigations. • Propose low‑cost compromises such as an explicit privacy notice, an extended response window, and a deletion schedule. • Escalate to oversight bodies, including the privacy officer, ethics committee, or data governance board. • Refuse to endorse misleading reports that claim anonymity when it cannot be guaranteed. • Insist on pre‑aggregated datasets for analysis that meet minimum group thresholds.

Practical Checklist

• Authenticate then anonymize using one‑time tokens. • Inform clearly with explicit limits of anonymity. • Minimize collected data and avoid unnecessary metadata. • Limit retention with automated deletion. • Aggregate safely and enforce minimum group sizes. • Audit regularly and publish summaries. • Secure storage with encryption and RBAC. • Prepare for fabrication with verification procedures. • Prevent unfair relabeling through policy and consent. • Communicate outcomes and publish follow‑up action plans.

Conclusion

Requiring authentication while promising anonymity creates a structural contradiction that erodes trust and can enable misuse. The ethical path is to be transparent about technical limits, to separate eligibility checks from response collection, to minimize and promptly delete identifying metadata, and to govern survey data with clear policies and independent oversight. Implementing the prioritized steps above will restore respondent trust, improve data quality, and reduce legal and reputational risk. Designing surveys ethically is not an obstacle to decision-making; it is the foundation for reliable, actionable insights that genuinely improve courses while protecting the people who provide the feedback.

Mathjax

Las ecuaciones matemáticas se pueden renderizar usando la sintaxis de Mathjax con soporte de símbolos AMS.

Opcionalmente, habilita esto por página agregando mathjax: true a tu frontmatter.

Luego, usa $$ ... $$ en una línea por sí sola para renderizar una ecuación de bloque:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

La versión sin procesar es:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con \\( ... \\) , como \( x^n / y \) . ¡Es fácil!

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con `\\( ... \\)` , como \\( x^n / y \\) . ¡Es fácil!

La version brute est:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Écrivez des équations en ligne avec \\( ... \\) , comme \( x^n / y \) . C’est facile!

Los emoji se pueden habilitar en un proyecto Hugo de varias maneras. :zap:

Soporte de Emoji

La función emojify se puede llamar directamente en las plantillas o Shortcodes en línea.

Para habilitar emoji globalmente, establece enableEmoji en true en la configuración de tu sitio y luego puedes escribir códigos abreviados de emoji directamente en los archivos de contenido; por ejemplo:

La Hoja de trucos de Emoji es una referencia útil para los códigos abreviados de emoji.

N.B. Los pasos anteriores habilitan los caracteres y secuencias de emoji del Estándar Unicode en Hugo, sin embargo, la representación de estos glifos depende del navegador y la plataforma. Para estilizar los emoji, puedes usar una fuente de emoji de terceros o una pila de fuentes; por ejemplo:

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Soporte de Emoji

content:
Les emoji peuvent être activés dans un projet Hugo de plusieurs façons. :zap:

Support des Emoji

La fonction emojify peut être appelée directement dans les modèles ou les Shortcodes en ligne.

Pour activer les emoji globalement, définissez enableEmoji sur true dans la configuration de votre site, puis vous pouvez taper les codes abrégés d’emoji directement dans les fichiers de contenu; par exemple :

La Feuille de triche Emoji est une référence utile pour les codes abrégés d’emoji.

N.B. Les étapes ci-dessus activent les caractères et séquences emoji du standard Unicode dans Hugo, cependant le rendu de ces glyphes dépend du navigateur et de la plateforme. Pour styliser les emoji, vous pouvez utiliser une police emoji tierce ou une pile de polices; par exemple :

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}

Eurytus Hector, materna ipsumque ut Politen, nec, nate, ignari, vernum cohaesit sequitur. Vel malis latens indetectus caelum supremos missus, nec nil videt exiguo! Manifestum, exasperat nova tela, ora, decipit ipse; fuerat gelidis sua.

Mentefque cum suae pelle

Viscera tot vagina, mihi est Theseus! Obstruat pede! Concita tamen, cum et vulnera croceo aegide tetigere deducit. Est quis vultus fata, scelus paratae inter agmen spectat fuit, Thisbe vulnera vellet laniarat, arva paludibus!

Dixit exarsit nocens, dextra secum ira columbae: Proponas, in pinus et alis dea atque. Aeneas, certum possent, temeraria, umeris colla in quoque. Interea fugit inpediunt nocet.

Praecipites nescio Titania tetigit, nunc volentem sua pectora nec, exstinctum Pylius undas. Agro. Dumque in est moenia vidi, vinci dat acervos precatur bracchia et modo et quos cum illas solvet, dum fecit.
